content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 15 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 212–213 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_015_page_001.jpg`, `DEU_015_page_002.jpg` | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/DEU_015_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`

---

### The Year of Release — Cancel Every Debt (vv. 1–6)

**1** At the end of every seven years you must cancel debts. **2** This is how the cancellation works: every creditor who has made a loan to a neighbor shall release the claim — he shall not press his neighbor or brother for repayment, because the LORD's release has been declared. **3** You may press a foreigner to pay, but whatever your fellow Israelite owes you, you must release. **4** There should be no poor people among you — for the LORD your God will richly bless you in the land he is giving you as an inheritance to possess — **5** if only you fully obey the LORD your God and are careful to follow all these commands I am giving you today. **6** For the LORD your God will bless you as he has promised: you will lend to many nations but borrow from none; you will rule over many nations but none will rule over you.

---

### Do Not Close Your Heart (vv. 7–11)

**7** If anyone among you is poor — one of your fellow Israelites in any of the towns in the land the LORD your God is giving you — do not be hard-hearted or tight-fisted toward them. **8** Instead, open your hand wide and freely lend them whatever they need. **9** Watch yourself carefully: do not let this wicked thought take hold of you — "The seventh year, the year of release, is near" — so that you look at your poor neighbor with a cold eye and give them nothing. If they cry out to the LORD against you, you will be found guilty of sin. **10** Give freely and do not resent it when you do, because the LORD your God will bless you in all your work and in everything you undertake. **11** There will always be poor people in the land. That is exactly why I am commanding you: open your hand wide to your fellow Israelites who are poor and needy in your land.

---

### The Hebrew Servant — Set Free in the Seventh Year (vv. 12–18)

**12** If a fellow Hebrew — man or woman — sells themselves to you and serves you six years, you must release them as a free person in the seventh year. **13** And when you release them, do not send them away empty-handed. **14** Supply them generously from your flock, your threshing floor, and your winepress. Give to them as the LORD your God has given to you. **15** Remember that you were a slave in Egypt and the LORD your God redeemed you. That is why I am giving you this command today.

**16** But suppose your servant says to you, "I do not want to leave" — because they love you and your household and are well off with you. **17** Then take an awl and push it through their earlobe into the door, and they will be your servant for life. Do the same for your female servant. **18** Do not consider it a hardship to set your servant free, because their service to you has been worth twice what you would have paid a hired hand — and the LORD your God will bless you in everything you do.

---

### Firstborn Animals Belong to the LORD (vv. 19–23)

**19** Set apart for the LORD your God every firstborn male from your herd and your flock. Do not put a firstborn ox to work and do not shear a firstborn sheep. **20** Each year you and your household shall eat them in the presence of the LORD your God at the place he will choose. **21** If an animal has a defect — is lame or blind, or has any serious flaw — you must not sacrifice it to the LORD your God. **22** Eat it in your own town instead: the ceremonially unclean and the clean alike may eat it, as if it were a gazelle or a deer. **23** But you must not eat the blood — pour it out on the ground like water.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 1 — "cancel debts"**: Hebrew *shemitah* — release, dropping, letting fall. This is not a payment plan or a restructuring — it is cancellation. Every seven years the debt slate is wiped clean. PNEUMA renders it plainly: "cancel debts." The economic radicalism of this command should not be domesticated.

**v. 2 — "the LORD's release has been declared"**: Hebrew *shemitah la-YHWH* (a release belonging to/for the LORD). Dynamic: the release is framed as an act belonging to God — Israel participates in a divine rhythm of liberation. The year of release echoes the Exodus, the Sabbath, and anticipates the Jubilee.

**vv. 4–5 — Tension**: Verse 4 says there should be no poor among you; verse 11 says there will always be poor among you. PNEUMA preserves this honest tension. The ideal (v. 4) is God's intention if the commands are obeyed. The reality (v. 11) is that they never will be perfectly obeyed — so ongoing generosity remains commanded. Jesus quotes v. 11 (John 12:8), not to make peace with poverty but to refocus the moment.

**v. 7 — "hard-hearted or tight-fisted"**: Hebrew *lo' t'amets et l'vav'cha v'lo' tiqpotz et yadcha* (do not harden your heart, do not shut your hand). Two vivid Hebrew idioms rendered dynamically. The heart clenches; the fist closes. PNEUMA's rendering makes the body language of stinginess visible.

**v. 8 — "open your hand wide"**: Hebrew *patoach tiftach et yadcha* — an intensive doubled verb construction, emphatic. "Open your hand wide" captures the intensity. This is not reluctant charity but extravagant, embodied generosity.

**v. 9 — "wicked thought"**: Hebrew *d'var beliya'al* (a word/thing of worthlessness). Dynamic: "wicked thought" — the text is warning against internal rationalization before it becomes action. Calculating that the release year is approaching and therefore refusing to lend is portrayed as morally corrupt reasoning, not sensible finance.

**v. 9 — "cold eye"**: Hebrew *ra'ah b'ra' ayin'cha* (your eye will be evil toward them). The "evil eye" idiom in Hebrew denotes miserly, withholding behavior. Dynamic: "look at your poor neighbor with a cold eye."

**v. 11 — "That is exactly why"**: PNEUMA inserts this for rhetorical clarity — "because there will always be poor" is not a resignation to injustice but the stated ground for ongoing obligation. The permanence of poverty is reason for permanent generosity, not permanent indifference.

**vv. 12–15 — "Sell themselves"**: The Hebrew indentured servant (*eved ivri*) is not chattel slavery but a system of debt-service: a person could enter temporary servitude to pay off debts, with a guaranteed exit in year seven. The command in v. 14 — "do not send them away empty-handed" — ensures the newly freed person has economic footing. This is structured exit from poverty, not just freedom.

**v. 15 — "Remember that you were a slave in Egypt"**: The Exodus is the ethical engine of Deuteronomy's social legislation. Every command about servants, debt, and the poor traces back to Israel's own experience of vulnerability and divine rescue. Memory is meant to produce empathy.

**v. 17 — "awl through the earlobe"**: The ear-piercing ceremony was a public act at the threshold of the household — the doorpost, the same place Passover blood was applied. The servant who chooses to remain becomes permanently marked as belonging to this household. PNEUMA preserves the ritual vividly.

**vv. 19–23 — Firstborn animals**: The firstborn of every animal belongs to God — not to be exploited for human profit (no working the ox, no shearing the sheep). They are to be eaten as a sacred meal. If defective, they may be eaten locally like any other game — but never as an offering. The blood prohibition (v. 23) applies as always: life belongs to God. — *PNEUMA*
